reasoning:
The current value of the S&P 500 3-Month Volatility Index (VXV) stands at 22.48, which is historically elevated. Over the last two months, VXV has been on a downtrend following a sharp spike in early April 2025 (reaching 35.71). Mean-reversion generally applies to volatility indices, and returning to a more typical 15-18 range is highly probable over the coming months.

The FRED trend analysis indicates a linear downward trend and models the probability of a near-term increase at 32-33%. Additionally, evidence from search shows a strong seasonal tendency for volatility to cool off into late June. Therefore, my expectation for the shortest horizon (June 29) aligns with the model's estimate of 33%. 

As the forecasting horizon expands to July, September, and December, two competing factors come into play: (a) mean reversion theoretically makes staying above 22.48 less likely in the long run, pulling the pure mathematical probability down, but (b) fundamental uncertainty regarding black swan events, elections, or economic shocks grows over time, increasing the variance of the underlying distribution. Following basic forecasting principles and the specific instructions to increase uncertainty (pull probabilities toward 0.5) over longer horizons, I am stepping the probabilities up gradually toward 0.5 for the distant resolution dates.
